data.name: Rob R. de Ruyter van Steveninck
Belén M. Montabes de la Cruz

data.title: Mental representation without neural representation: Understanding the evidence
data.abstract: The authors challenge the widespread assumption in neuroscience that neural states and processes function as representations. While they accept the reality of cognitive representations at the personal/psychological level, they argue that the evidence typically cited for neural representations (e.g., selective responsiveness, informational correlation) is insufficient. Critiquing recent frameworks like that of Pohl et al. (2024), they contend that such evidence is equally compatible with non-representational causal roles. They advocate for a more rigorous epistemological approach that focuses on how neural information is consumed by downstream processes rather than how it is triggered by stimuli.
data.thesis: Neural states do not function as representations; the standard evidence (selective responsiveness, informational correlation) is insufficient to establish representational status and is equally compatible with non-representational causal roles.

data.name: Neural Representation
data.description: The claim that neural states and processes function as representations of external states of affairs

data.name: Mereological Fallacy
data.description: The faulty inference that because a global system (the mind/person) represents, its component parts (neurons) must also be representational

data.name: Job-Description Challenge
data.description: The requirement to demonstrate that a neural state performs a functional role sufficiently similar to the stand-in or informer role characteristic of genuine representations

data.name: Afferent Evidence
data.description: Evidence concerning the triggering etiology of a neural state -- how it is caused by stimuli

data.name: Efferent Evidence
data.description: Evidence concerning the downstream impact and consumption of a neural state's information

data.name: Neural Representation Attribution Error
data.description: The systematic mistake of assuming a representational role for neural activity and subsequently using representational language to describe what are likely non-representational causal processes

data.name: Surrogative Reasoning
data.description: The capacity to make inferences based on structural relations between representational elements themselves, required for genuine representational status

data.name: Prime Principle of Confirmation
data.description: The epistemological rule that evidence E supports a representational hypothesis H1 over a non-representational hypothesis H2 only if E is more likely given H1 than H2

data.title: The dark path to eternal life: Machiavellianism predicts approval of mind upload technology
data.abstract: Mind upload, making a digital copy of one's brain, is a part of the transhumanistic dream of eternal life and the end of suffering. It is also perceived as a viable route toward artificial general intelligence (AGI). However, AI safety research has alerted to one major risk in creating AGI by mind upload: namely, that mind upload technology could appeal primarily to callous and selfish individuals who then abuse this technology for their personal gain--and, potentially, at a considerable cost to the welfare of humankind. Therefore, it is important to understand whether people's acceptance of mind upload is associated with pathological and/or antisocial traits. To this end, the present research examined whether individual differences in Dark Triad traits predict attitudes toward mind upload in a sample of 1007 English-speaking adults. A pre-registered structural equation model revealed that Machiavellianism (but not psychopathy) was associated with favourable views about mind upload, both directly and indirectly through utilitarian moral attitudes. These results therefore substantiate the concerns voiced by AI safety researchers--namely, that mind upload technology could be adopted disproportionately by individuals with an antisocial personality.

data.title: Microtubules are 'Fractal Time Crystals': Implications for Life and Consciousness
data.abstract: Microtubules are cylindrical lattice polymers of the protein tubulin which organise purposeful activities in brain neurons and all eukaryotic cells. Here we report that microtubules behave as 'fractal time crystals', characterised by self-similar resonance oscillations, 'clocks within clocks' in a nested hierarchy of similar dynamics ('triplets-of-triplets') which repeat in hertz, kilohertz, megahertz, gigahertz, and terahertz frequencies. Megahertz triplets are detectable from human scalp beyond traditional EEG frequencies, and are suppressed by general anaesthetics, indicating a connection to consciousness. The Orch OR theory proposes that consciousness results from quantum state reductions in microtubules consistent with time crystal behaviour.

data.title: 20260312_functional_recovery_adult_murine_hippocampus_after_cryopreservation_by_vitrification
data.abstract: This paper demonstrates short-term functional recovery of adult murine hippocampus after cryopreservation by vitrification, both in brain slices and whole brains in situ. Key hippocampal features like structural integrity, metabolic responsiveness, neuronal excitability, synaptic transmission, and plasticity (including LTP) were preserved. These findings extend biophysical tolerance limits for brain tissue from hypothermic to cryogenic ranges, establishing a protocol for viable neural tissue storage.

data.title: Ultraviolet Superradiance from Mega-Networks of Tryptophan in Biological Architectures
data.abstract: This paper analyzes the cooperative effects induced by UV excitation in biologically relevant tryptophan (Trp) mega-networks, predicting strongly superradiant states due to collective interactions in microtubule architectures. This leads to an enhancement of fluorescence quantum yield (QY), confirmed experimentally, and demonstrates the effect's persistence even with physiological disorder. The work highlights the coexistence of bright and dark states across many orders of magnitude in these Trp lattices.
data.thesis: Mega-networks of tryptophan (Trp) in biological architectures exhibit collective ultraviolet superradiance, leading to enhanced fluorescence quantum yields (QYs). This superradiant behavior is predicted by theoretical and computational models for various hierarchical tubulin structures and is experimentally confirmed by observed QY increases in tubulin and microtubules, demonstrating its robustness to thermal equilibrium and structural disorder.

data.claim: Mega-networks of Trp in biological architectures (e.g., microtubules, centrioles, neuronal bundles) exhibit a collective optical response in the UV region, forming strongly superradiant states.
data.support: Theoretical analysis using a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian in the single-excitation manifold, diagonalizing the matrix for various Trp arrangements (MTs, centrioles, MT bundles) to determine radiative decay rates and superradiance enhancement factors.

data.claim: The formation of strongly superradiant states in Trp mega-networks leads to an enhancement of the fluorescence quantum yield (QY).
data.support: QY predictions calculated from thermal averages of radiative and nonradiative decay rates, showing sigmoid-like increases in QY with increasing Trp network size (Figures 3a, 3b, 3c).

data.claim: The predicted QY enhancements due to superradiance in Trp mega-networks are robust to static disorder and thermal equilibrium.
data.support: Simulations including time-independent fluctuations in Trp excitation energies (disorder strength W), showing QY is largely unaffected even at W = 200 cm⁻¹ (room temperature energy) and observable at 1000 cm⁻¹ disorder (Figure 4). Theoretical explanation for robustness based on dipole strength distribution.

data.claim: Experimental measurements confirm that fluorescence QYs increase with increasing Trp network size in hierarchically organized tubulin structures.
data.support: Steady-state fluorescence QY measurements for Trp, tubulin dimers (TuD), and microtubules (MTs) in BRB80 buffer, showing a statistically significant increase in QY from TuD (10.6 ± 0.6%) to MTs (17.6* ± 2.1%) (Table 1), which is in qualitative agreement with computational predictions.

data.claim: Collective radiative processes in protein assemblies with mega-ensembles of Trp are the primary cause of the significant QY increases observed in MTs.
data.support: The observed QY increase from TuD to MTs, despite a decrease from Trp to TuD (suggesting nonradiative processes in TuD), implies that the increase in MTs is not due to decreased nonradiative decay but rather enhanced radiative processes, consistent with the superradiance model.

data.claim: Superradiant states in biological Trp mega-networks can coexist with subradiant states across many orders of magnitude in decay rates.
data.support: Analysis of the complex eigenvalues of the effective Hamiltonian, which yield both bright (superradiant) states with hundreds of femtoseconds decay and dark (subradiant) states with tens of seconds decay (implied by the range of Γ_j values and the abstract statement).

data.claim: The single-excitation manifold is a reasonable approximation for describing the collective optical response, especially given the biological milieu of ultraweak photon emissions.
The chosen La peak excitation at 280 nm is the relevant transition dipole moment for Trp.
The nonradiative decay rate (Γnr) for a single Trp (0.0193 cm⁻¹) is a suitable baseline for the model, and the model neglects additional nonradiative channels with increasing Trp network size.
Thermal equilibrium conditions (room temperature, kBT ≈ 207 cm⁻¹) are appropriate for analyzing the QY.
The protein structural models derived from PDB entries and virtual assembly accurately represent the biological geometries and Trp arrangements.

data.definition: The ratio of the number of emitted photons (per time and volume unit) relative to the number of absorbed ones. Equivalently, the QY can be defined in terms of the radiative decay rate Γ and the nonradiative decay rate Γnr: QY = Γ/(Γ + Γnr).
Represents all of the physical and chemical processes involved in the interaction between the chromophore network and the surrounding protein(s) or solvent.
The internal conversion rate constant, as a component of Γnr.
The intersystem crossing rate constant, as a component of Γnr.

data.term: Rate constant due to quenching or photochemical reactions (Γreact)
data.definition: The rate constant due to quenching or photochemical reactions, as a component of Γnr.

data.term: Thermal average for the decay rate ((Γ)th)

data.definition: QYs = (Fs/Fr)(nr/ns)QYr, where s and r represent the examined sample and reference, respectively. F is the integrated fluorescence area and n is the refractive index of the respective solution.
Determined by a = 1-10⁻ᴬ, with A being the optical density at the absorption wavelength of the absorption band.
Parameter representing time-independent fluctuations in the on-site energies of each Trp transition dipole using a random uniform distribution of energies within the range given by Eo ± W/2.
Cm,n = sin(πn/(N+1)) sin(2π[m/3]/9), where [x] denotes the ceiling function (i.e., the nearest integer larger than x).

data.question: Inability to prepare solutions with exact concentrations for samples.
data.context: Could affect the precision and reliability of experimental QY measurements.

data.question: Issues with scattered light and sample purity for larger Trp assemblies.
data.context: Prevented QY measurements on larger biological architectures beyond tubulin dimers and microtubules.

data.question: Inability to measure the radiative rate of fluorescence directly from fluorescence decays.
data.context: Requires measurements over a wide temporal range (femtoseconds to nanoseconds) not possible with a single experimental setup, and fluorescence decays are nonexponential, making it difficult to obtain a reliable radiative rate.

data.question: The theoretical model does not account for additional nonradiative channels.
data.context: Could lead to discrepancies between theoretical predictions and experimental observations, especially for large Trp ensembles.

data.question: Computational resources limited the Hilbert space dimensions for numerical simulations.
data.context: Restricted the maximum axon lengths that could be simulated directly, leading to diminishing maximum axon lengths as the number of MTs increased.

data.question: The link among cellular metabolic activity, ultraweak photon emissions (UPEs), and Trp network optical dynamics.
data.context: This link remains unclear, representing a critical gap in knowledge regarding biophotonic signaling and oxidative metabolism.

data.question: The implications of large Trp architectures for the photophysical control of biosystems.
data.context: While Trp's roles as a metabolic precursor and fluorescent reporter are known, its broader implications for photophysical control in large biological architectures are largely unexplored.

data.question: Whether axons might serve as waveguides for superradiant emitters in the brain.
data.context: The proposal of confining superradiant optical modes in waveguides to extend emitter interactions raises the tantalizing possibility of long-range quantum communication in the brain.

data.question: Whether mega-networks of indole from Trp and neuromelanin can aid in 'internal' UV energy downconversion and funneling in the brain.
data.context: This question relates to potential photoprotective roles and energy management mechanisms in the brain.

data.question: The in vivo exploitability of the collective ultraviolet response from Trp mega-networks.
data.context: The observed collective superradiant eigenmodes and their manifestation in increased QYs suggest potential for in vivo applications, but how this would be achieved is an open question.

data.question: Complement fluorescence QY measurements with lifetime measurements.
Develop more robust models to account for exciton-phonon couplings in protein scaffold deformations and optical pumping of mechanical modes in nonequilibrium structural organization and assembly.
Augment artificial light-harvesting devices using the UV superradiance response of Trp mega-networks to extend and enhance the spectral band of absorption.
The small magnitudes of spectral shifts and sharpening observed in MTs ostensibly weigh against their physiological significance, yet they lead to robust consequences on the QY.
data.context: The seemingly minor spectral changes and their significant, robust impact on quantum yield.

data.question: Thermalization significantly competes with enhancements to the QY from collective effects, yet these effects are not eliminated and persist.
data.context: The mitigating influence of thermal equilibrium and the observed survival of QY enhancements.

data.question: Static disorder strongly suppresses the superradiance enhancement factor (e.g., from ~3600 to ~20 for a centriole), yet the QY values remain very close and robust to this disorder.
data.context: The dramatic reduction in the superradiance enhancement factor due to disorder and the robustness of the quantum yield.

data.question: Fundamental challenges of coherent quantum optical information transfer at ambient temperatures due to disorder, yet fluorescence QY measurements suggest these effects survive.
data.context: The theoretical difficulties and quenching effects of disorder on coherent transfer, and the experimental evidence for the survival of QY enhancements at thermal equilibrium.

data.question: The QY decreases from Trp to TuD (suggesting increased nonradiative decay), but then significantly increases from TuD to MTs (which would imply decreased nonradiative decay), despite the model not accounting for additional nonradiative channels in large ensembles.
data.context: The observed QY trends across different hierarchical structures (Trp -> TuD -> MTs) and the intuitive expectation of nonradiative decay changes, especially given the model's limitations.
